flashcards.q: What is grouped data?
flashcards.a: Data that is organized into class intervals with their corresponding frequencies.

flashcards.q: Define class interval.
flashcards.a: A range of values used to group data; defined by lower and upper class limits.

flashcards.q: What is the class mark?
flashcards.a: The midpoint of a class interval; calculated as (Lower limit + Upper limit) / 2.

flashcards.q: Define class width (h).
flashcards.a: The difference between the upper and lower limits of a class interval: h = Upper limit - Lower limit.

flashcards.q: What is frequency?
flashcards.a: The number of observations falling in a particular class interval.

flashcards.q: What is cumulative frequency?
flashcards.a: The sum of frequencies of a class and all classes preceding it; the running total of frequencies.

flashcards.q: State the direct method formula for mean.
flashcards.a: Mean (x̄) = Σ(fi × xi) / Σfi, where xi are class marks and fi are frequencies.

flashcards.q: State the assumed mean method formula.
flashcards.a: Mean (x̄) = a + Σ(fi × di) / Σfi, where a is the assumed mean and di = xi − a are deviations.

flashcards.q: State the step deviation method formula.
flashcards.a: Mean (x̄) = a + [Σ(fi × ui) / Σfi] × h, where ui = (xi − a) / h are step deviations.

flashcards.q: What is the assumed mean?
flashcards.a: An arbitrary class mark chosen as a reference point to simplify calculations; typically chosen from a class near the middle of the distribution.

flashcards.q: What is deviation (di)?
flashcards.a: The difference between a class mark and the assumed mean: di = xi − a.

flashcards.q: What is step deviation (ui)?
flashcards.a: The deviation divided by class width: ui = (xi − a) / h or ui = di / h.

flashcards.q: When is the step deviation method most useful?
flashcards.a: When all classes have equal width and class marks are large numbers, minimizing arithmetic complexity.

flashcards.q: State the mode formula for grouped data.
flashcards.a: Mode = l + [(f1 − f0) / (2f1 − f0 − f2)] × h, where l = lower limit of modal class, f1 = modal class frequency, f0 = preceding class frequency, f2 = succeeding class frequency.

flashcards.q: What is the modal class?
flashcards.a: The class interval with the highest frequency in the distribution.

flashcards.q: State the median formula for grouped data.
flashcards.a: Median = l + [(n/2 − CF) / f] × h, where l = lower median class limit, n = total frequency, CF = cumulative frequency before median class, f = median class frequency.

flashcards.q: What is the median class?
flashcards.a: The class interval where the cumulative frequency first exceeds or equals n/2.

flashcards.q: How do you identify the median class?
flashcards.a: Find where cumulative frequency ≥ n/2 for the first time; that class is the median class.

flashcards.q: Define an ogive.
flashcards.a: A cumulative frequency curve used to visualize cumulative frequencies and find medians or percentiles graphically.

flashcards.q: What is a less than ogive?
flashcards.a: A curve where points (upper class limit, cumulative frequency) are plotted; the curve rises from left to right.

flashcards.q: What is a greater than ogive?
flashcards.a: A curve where points (lower class limit, cumulative frequency from right) are plotted; the curve falls from left to right.

flashcards.q: How do you find the median graphically from a less than ogive?
flashcards.a: Locate n/2 on the cumulative frequency axis, draw a horizontal line to the curve, then drop a perpendicular to the x-axis. The x-coordinate is the median.

flashcards.q: State the empirical relationship between mean, median, and mode.
flashcards.a: Mode = 3 × Median − 2 × Mean, or equivalently, Median = Mode + (1/3)(Mean − Mode).

flashcards.q: When do the mean, median, and mode coincide?
flashcards.a: For a perfectly symmetrical distribution, mean = median = mode.

flashcards.q: What does it mean if mode > median > mean?
flashcards.a: The distribution is negatively skewed (left-skewed) with a longer left tail.

flashcards.q: What does it mean if mean > median > mode?
flashcards.a: The distribution is positively skewed (right-skewed) with a longer right tail.

flashcards.q: What is skewness in a distribution?
flashcards.a: Skewness is the asymmetry of a distribution; it indicates whether the distribution has a longer tail on one side.

flashcards.q: Why is the class mark used to represent a class in grouped data?
flashcards.a: It is assumed that all observations in a class are concentrated at the class mark, making it a representative value for that class.

flashcards.q: What is the advantage of the assumed mean method over the direct method?
flashcards.a: It reduces computational effort when class marks are large numbers by working with smaller deviation values.

flashcards.q: What is the advantage of the step deviation method over the assumed mean method?
flashcards.a: Step deviations are integers (when a is chosen as a class mark and h is constant), further simplifying calculations.

flashcards.q: What condition must be met for all three mean calculation methods to give identical results?
flashcards.a: All three methods are mathematically equivalent; they will always give the same result regardless of the assumed mean or class width chosen.

flashcards.q: How does grouping data affect the accuracy of statistical measures?
flashcards.a: Grouping causes a loss of information; the exact values are replaced by class marks, so calculated values are approximations of the true measures.

flashcards.q: In the mode formula, what does (2f1 − f0 − f2) represent?
flashcards.a: It represents the difference in the rates of change of frequency on either side of the modal class, helping locate the mode more precisely within the class.

flashcards.q: What does the numerator (f1 − f0) represent in the mode formula?
flashcards.a: It represents the excess frequency of the modal class over the preceding class, indicating the direction and magnitude of concentration.

flashcards.q: How do you convert a greater than ogive to a less than ogive?
flashcards.a: The less than ogive is obtained by plotting the complement: if N is total frequency, plot (lower limit, N − CF from right) or equivalently (upper limit, CF from left).

flashcards.q: Can two class intervals have the same cumulative frequency?
flashcards.a: No; cumulative frequencies are monotonically increasing (or staying the same if a class has zero frequency), so each class has a unique cumulative frequency value.

flashcards.q: What is the relationship between frequency and cumulative frequency?
flashcards.a: Frequency of class i = CF(i) − CF(i−1), where CF(i) is cumulative frequency up to class i.

flashcards.q: Why must class intervals be continuous for median and mode calculations?
flashcards.a: The formulas assume continuous data without gaps; discontinuous intervals would violate the assumptions and lead to incorrect results.

flashcards.q: In the median formula, why do we use n/2 instead of (n+1)/2?
flashcards.a: For grouped data, n/2 represents the position of the median in the cumulative frequency distribution, whereas (n+1)/2 is used for ungrouped data with discrete positioning.

flashcards.q: What is the significance of the point where the less than and greater than ogives intersect?
flashcards.a: The x-coordinate of the intersection point is the median of the distribution.

flashcards.q: How do you handle class intervals with different widths when calculating mean?
flashcards.a: The direct method still works; use class marks as xi. However, step deviation method cannot be used directly without adjusting for varying widths.

flashcards.q: What does it mean if the mode falls outside the modal class?
flashcards.a: This indicates an error in calculation or that the distribution is multimodal; the mode formula assumes a single, well-defined modal class.

flashcards.q: How is the percentile related to cumulative frequency?
flashcards.a: The pth percentile is the value below which p% of observations fall; found using cumulative frequencies at (n × p / 100).
